steps.message:
<permissions instructions>
Filesystem sandboxing defines which files can be read or written. `sandbox_mode` is `danger-full-access`: No filesystem sandboxing - all commands are permitted. Network access is enabled.
Approval policy is currently never. Do not provide the `sandbox_permissions` for any reason, commands will be rejected.
</permissions instructions><skills_instructions>
## Skills
A skill is a set of instructions provided through a `SKILL.md` source. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and source locator. `file` locators are on the host filesystem, `environment resource` locators are owned by an execution environment, `orchestrator resource` locators are opaque non-filesystem resources, and `custom resource` locators use their provider's access mechanism.
### Available skills
- imagegen: Generate or edit raster images when the task benefits from AI-created bitmap visuals such as photos, illustrations, textures, sprites, mockups, or transparent-background cutouts. Use when Codex should create a brand-new image, transform an existing image, or derive visual variants from references, and the output should be a bitmap asset rather than repo-native code or vector. Do not use when the task is better handled by editing existing SVG/vector/code-native assets, extending an established icon or logo system, or building the visual directly in HTML/CSS/canvas. (file: /tmp/codex-home/skills/.system/imagegen/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs, asks about Codex itself or choosing Codex surfaces, needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or model upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; use OpenAI docs MCP tools for non-Codex docs questions, use the Codex manual helper first for broad Codex self-knowledge, and restrict fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /tmp/codex-home/skills/.system/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- plugin-creator: Create and scaffold plugin directories for Codex with a required `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, optional plugin folders/files, valid manifest defaults, and personal-marketplace entries by default. Use when Codex needs to create a new personal plugin, add optional plugin structure, generate or update marketplace entries for plugin ordering and availability metadata, or update an existing local plugin during development with the CLI-driven cachebuster and reinstall flow. (file: /tmp/codex-home/skills/.system/plugin-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /tmp/codex-home/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /tmp/codex-home/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + source locator). `file` entries live on the host filesystem, `environment resource` entries are owned by their execution environment, `orchestrator resource` entries must be accessed through `skills.list` and `skills.read`, and `custom resource` entries use their provider's access mechanism.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or its source can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, the main agent must read its `SKILL.md` completely before taking task actions. For a `file` entry, open the listed path. For an `environment resource`, use the filesystem of the owning environment. For an `orchestrator resource`, call `skills.list` with `{"authority":{"kind":"orchestrator"}}`, select the matching package, and pass its `main_resource` to `skills.read`. If a read is truncated or paginated, continue until EOF.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references another resource, use the same access mechanism. Resolve relative paths against a filesystem-backed skill directory. For orchestrator skills, pass the exact referenced resource identifier with the same authority and package to `skills.read`; do not treat `skill://` identifiers as filesystem paths.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, use its routing instructions to identify the resources required for the task. The main agent must read each required instruction or reference file itself before acting on it. Do not delegate reading, summarizing, or interpreting skill instructions to a subagent. Subagents may still perform task work when the selected skill allows it.
  4) For filesystem-backed skills, prefer running or patching provided scripts instead of retyping large code blocks. For orchestrator skills, use `skills.read` and the available tools; do not invent a local path.
  5) Reuse provided assets or templates through the same source access mechanism instead of recreating them.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Progressive disclosure applies to selecting relevant files, not partially reading a selected instruction file. Do not load unrelated references, scripts, or assets.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
</skills_instructions>

# Task description

Several concurrency and unbounded-growth issues affect the work-stealing pool and supporting structures. Address the following:

- **Data race in the work-stealing deque** (`pkg/queue/deque.go`): concurrent owner push/pop and thief steal operations race on shared state. Make the deque safe under simultaneous access from the owning goroutine and multiple stealers, preserving the existing LIFO-owner / FIFO-thief semantics and the public API.
- **Unbounded probe history** in SWIM membership (`pkg/membership/swim.go`): failure-detection probe history grows without limit. Cap retained history to a fixed bound, discarding the oldest entries so memory stays stable during long runs without altering failure-detection decisions.
- **Unbounded event log** in the network simulator (`pkg/sim/network.go`): the discrete-event log accumulates indefinitely. Bound its size while keeping recently recorded events available for inspection.

Behavior visible to existing callers (queue ordering, membership state transitions, simulation results) must remain unchanged. Do not introduce external dependencies.

# Test guidelines

Run `go test ./` for the visible check. Add or extend tests under `pkg/queue` and `pkg/membership` to cover the new guarantees: the deque tests should exercise concurrent push/pop/steal under `-race` and assert no lost or duplicated items; the membership tests should confirm probe history stays within its bound across many probe cycles. Validate the full suite with `go test ./... -race -count=1`.

# Lint guidelines

Run `go vet ./...` and `gofmt -l .` (expect no listed files) before finishing.

# Style guidelines

You are already on the correct starting snapshot. Create your branch from this state. Do not rebase or start from master, main, or any other branch.
I’ll inspect the repository structure and current branch first, then read the queue, membership, and simulator implementations plus existing tests so the fixes match local patterns.

steps.message: A few concrete issues are visible now: the deque itself uses a mutex, but `WorkStealingPool.Steal` mutates `stolen` while holding only an `RLock`, which is a race under concurrent steals. I’m going to tighten that and add the requested bounded-history helpers/tests rather than changing caller-facing behavior.

The code changes are small and localized: I’ll add append helpers that enforce fixed retention for probe history and simulator events, switch the steal counter to an atomic counter, and add focused concurrency/history tests. The deque API and ordering behavior stay as-is.

arguments.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /testbed/frontiercode-repo/pkg/queue/deque.go
@@
-import "sync"
+import (
+	"sync"
+	"sync/atomic"
+)
@@
 type WorkStealingPool struct {
 	mu     sync.RWMutex
 	deques map[string]*Deque
-	stolen uint64
+	stolen atomic.Uint64
 }
@@
 		item, ok := d.StealTop()
 		if ok {
-			p.stolen++
+			p.stolen.Add(1)
 			return item, workerID, true
 		}
 	}
 	return nil, "", false
@@
 func (p *WorkStealingPool) StolenCount() uint64 {
-	p.mu.RLock()
-	defer p.mu.RUnlock()
-	return p.stolen
+	return p.stolen.Load()
 }
*** End Patch

     1	package membership

     3	import (
     4		"math/rand"
     5		"sync"

     8	
     9	const maxProbeHistory = 1024
    10	
    11	// SWIMConfig holds parameters for the SWIM failure detector.
    12	type SWIMConfig struct {
    13		// ProbeInterval is how often to probe a random member.
    14		ProbeInterval time.Duration
    15		// ProbeTimeout is how long to wait for a direct ack.
    16		ProbeTimeout time.Duration
    17		// IndirectChecks is the number of indirect probes to send.
    18		IndirectChecks int
    19		// SuspicionTimeout is how long a member stays suspect before being declared dead.
    20		SuspicionTimeout time.Duration
    21	}
    22	
    23	// DefaultSWIMConfig returns reasonable defaults.
    24	func DefaultSWIMConfig() SWIMConfig {
    25		return SWIMConfig{
    26			ProbeInterval:    time.Second,
    27			ProbeTimeout:     500 * time.Millisecond,
    28			IndirectChecks:   3,
    29			SuspicionTimeout: 5 * time.Second,
    30		}
    31	}
    32	
    33	// ProbeResult represents the outcome of a ping probe.
    34	type ProbeResult int
    35	
    36	const (
    37		ProbeAck     ProbeResult = iota // Direct ack received
    38		ProbeNack                       // No response
    39		ProbeTimeout                    // Timed out
    40	)
    41	
    42	// ProbeFunc is a callback that simulates or performs a ping probe to a target.
    43	// Returns ProbeAck if the target responds, ProbeNack/ProbeTimeout otherwise.
    44	type ProbeFunc func(target string) ProbeResult
    45	
    46	// IndirectProbeFunc asks a mediator to probe the target on our behalf.
    47	type IndirectProbeFunc func(mediator, target string) ProbeResult
    48	
    49	// SWIMDetector implements the SWIM protocol for failure detection.
    50	// It runs probe rounds, manages suspicion timers, and updates the member list.
    51	type SWIMDetector struct {
    52		mu           sync.Mutex
    53		config       SWIMConfig
    54		memberList   *MemberList
    55		suspicionMap map[string]time.Time // nodeID → when suspicion started
    56		probeFunc    ProbeFunc
    57		indirectFunc IndirectProbeFunc
    58		rng          *rand.Rand
    59		roundCount   uint64
    60		probeHistory []ProbeEvent
    61	}
    62	
    63	// ProbeEvent records the outcome of a single probe.
    64	type ProbeEvent struct {
    65		Round     uint64
    66		Target    string
    67		Result    ProbeResult
    68		Indirect  bool
    69		Timestamp time.Time
    70	}
    71	
    72	// NewSWIMDetector creates a failure detector for the given membership list.
    73	func NewSWIMDetector(ml *MemberList, config SWIMConfig, probeFn ProbeFunc, indirectFn IndirectProbeFunc, seed int64) *SWIMDetector {
    74		return &SWIMDetector{
    75			config:       config,
    76			memberList:   ml,
    77			suspicionMap: make(map[string]time.Time),
    78			probeFunc:    probeFn,
    79			indirectFunc: indirectFn,
    80			rng:          rand.New(rand.NewSource(seed)),
    81			probeHistory: make([]ProbeEvent, 0, maxProbeHistory),
    82		}
    83	}
    84	
    85	// RunProbeRound executes one round of the SWIM protocol:
    86	// 1. Select a random alive member to probe
    87	// 2. Send a direct ping
    88	// 3. If no ack, send indirect pings through k mediators
    89	// 4. If still no ack, mark as suspect
    90	// 5. Check suspicion timeouts and declare dead members
    91	func (sd *SWIMDetector) RunProbeRound() *ProbeEvent {
    92		sd.mu.Lock()
    93		defer sd.mu.Unlock()
    94		sd.roundCount++
    95	
    96		// Step 1: Select target
    97		alive := sd.memberList.AliveMembers(true)
    98		if len(alive) == 0 {
    99			return nil
   100		}
   101		targetIdx := sd.rng.Intn(len(alive))
   102		target := alive[targetIdx]
   103	
   104		// Step 2: Direct probe
   105		result := sd.probeFunc(target)
   106		event := &ProbeEvent{
   107			Round:     sd.roundCount,
   108			Target:    target,
   109			Result:    result,
   110			Indirect:  false,
   111			Timestamp: time.Now(),
   112		}
   113		sd.recordProbeEvent(*event)
   114	
   115		if result == ProbeAck {
   116			delete(sd.suspicionMap, target)
   117			return event
   118		}
   119	
   120		// Step 3: Indirect probes
   121		mediators := sd.selectMediators(alive, target, sd.config.IndirectChecks)
   122		indirectAck := false
   123		for _, med := range mediators {
   124			ir := sd.indirectFunc(med, target)
   125			sd.recordProbeEvent(ProbeEvent{
   126				Round:     sd.roundCount,
   127				Target:    target,
   128				Result:    ir,
   129				Indirect:  true,
   130				Timestamp: time.Now(),
   131			})
   132			if ir == ProbeAck {
   133				indirectAck = true
   134				break
   135			}
   136		}
   137	
   138		if indirectAck {
   139			delete(sd.suspicionMap, target)
   140			event.Result = ProbeAck
   141			return event
   142		}
   143	
   144		// Step 4: Mark as suspect
   145		if _, isSuspect := sd.suspicionMap[target]; !isSuspect {
   146			m := sd.memberList.Get(target)
   147			if m != nil {
   148				sd.memberList.Suspect(target, m.Incarnation)
   149				sd.suspicionMap[target] = time.Now()
   150			}
   151		}
   152	
   153		// Step 5: Check suspicion timeouts
   154		sd.checkSuspicionTimeouts()
   155	
   156		return event
   157	}
   158	
   159	func (sd *SWIMDetector) recordProbeEvent(event ProbeEvent) {
   160		if len(sd.probeHistory) == maxProbeHistory {
   161			copy(sd.probeHistory, sd.probeHistory[1:])
   162			sd.probeHistory[maxProbeHistory-1] = event
   163			return
   164		}
   165		sd.probeHistory = append(sd.probeHistory, event)
   166	}
   167	
   168	// checkSuspicionTimeouts promotes suspects to dead if their timeout has expired.
   169	func (sd *SWIMDetector) checkSuspicionTimeouts() {
   170		now := time.Now()
   171		for nodeID, started := range sd.suspicionMap {
   172			if now.Sub(started) >= sd.config.SuspicionTimeout {
   173				m := sd.memberList.Get(nodeID)
   174				if m != nil {
   175					sd.memberList.Dead(nodeID, m.Incarnation)
   176				}
   177				delete(sd.suspicionMap, nodeID)
   178			}
   179		}
   180	}
   181	
   182	// selectMediators picks up to n random alive members excluding target and self.
   183	func (sd *SWIMDetector) selectMediators(alive []string, target string, n int) []string {
   184		candidates := make([]string, 0, len(alive))
   185		for _, id := range alive {
   186			if id != target {
   187				candidates = append(candidates, id)
   188			}
   189		}
   190		if n > len(candidates) {

steps.message:
Implemented the concurrency and bounded-growth fixes.

Changed:
- [pkg/queue/deque.go](/testbed/frontiercode-repo/pkg/queue/deque.go:72): made `WorkStealingPool.stolen` atomic so concurrent steals no longer race.
- [pkg/membership/swim.go](/testbed/frontiercode-repo/pkg/membership/swim.go:9): capped SWIM probe history at `1024`, retaining newest entries.
- [pkg/sim/network.go](/testbed/frontiercode-repo/pkg/sim/network.go:12): capped simulator event log at `4096`, retaining recent events.
- Added concurrency/history/log bound tests in queue, membership, and sim packages.

Validation:
- `go test ./` was run and fails because the repository root has no Go files.
- `go test ./... -race -count=1` passes.
- `go vet ./...` passes.
- `gofmt -l .` now prints no files.

One environment note: `/testbed/frontiercode-repo` has no `.git` directory, so I could not create the requested branch.
